source_paper_bibtex: @article{Luo_2024, title={Distribution-Free Contextual Dynamic Pricing}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1369}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1369}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Luo, Yiyun and Sun, Will Wei and Liu, Yufeng}, year={2024}, month=feb, pages={599–618} }

Page 4 (Introduction, end of Abstract/Contributions discussion) and page 20 (Remark 2 following Theorem 1 in Section 3).

Because our problems involves both unknown linear parameter \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise distribution \(F\), we conjecture the obtained \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) rate is close to the optimal rate.

However, their constructed instances do not fit into our considered pricing problem with an additional linear structure \(x^\top\theta_0\). The additional unknown \(\theta_0\) makes the lower bound derivation harder and we leave it for future work.
title: Determine minimax regret rate for contextual dynamic pricing with unknown noise distribution
background_latex:
A seller repeatedly interacts with customers over periods \(t=1,\dots,T\). At time \(t\), a covariate (context) \(x_t\in\mathcal X\subset\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is observed, with \(\|x_t\|_\infty\le 1\). The customer's valuation is
\[
v_t = x_t^\top \theta_0 + z_t,
\]
where \(\theta_0\in\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is an unknown parameter (often assumed to satisfy \(\|\theta_0\|_1\le W\) for a known constant \(W\)), and \(\{z_t\}\) are i.i.d. draws from an unknown distribution with CDF \(F\). After the seller posts a price \(p_t>0\), the seller observes only the binary purchase indicator
\[
y_t = \mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}.
\]
The expected revenue from posting price \(p\) in context \(x\) is
\[
R(p;x) = p\,\mathbb P(v\ge p\mid x)= p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
Define the context-dependent optimal price
\[
p^*(x)\in \arg\max_{p>0} p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
The (pseudo-)regret over horizon \(T\) for a policy producing prices \(p_t\) is
\[
\mathcal R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T \Bigl( p^*(x_t)\bigl(1-F(p^*(x_t)-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) - p_t\bigl(1-F(p_t-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) \Bigr),
\]
and performance is measured by \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]\) over the randomness in the data and any policy randomization. The model is "distribution-free" in the sense that \(F\) is unknown and not restricted to a known parametric/log-concave family; the paper assumes regularity such as Lipschitz continuity of \(F\) in some analyses.
problem_definition_latex: Characterize the minimax-optimal regret rate in \(T\) for contextual dynamic pricing under the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), given only binary purchase feedback \(y_t=\mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}\). In particular, determine whether there exists a policy with expected cumulative regret \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) (up to logarithmic factors), or prove a matching lower bound (up to logs) showing \(\inf_{\pi}\sup_{\theta_0,F}\mathbb E_\pi[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) (or the correct exponent), over an appropriate class of admissible \(F\) consistent with the paper's assumptions.
latex:
A seller repeatedly interacts with customers over periods \(t=1,\dots,T\). At time \(t\), a covariate (context) \(x_t\in\mathcal X\subset\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is observed, with \(\|x_t\|_\infty\le 1\). The customer's valuation is
\[
v_t = x_t^\top \theta_0 + z_t,
\]
where \(\theta_0\in\mathbb R^{d_0}\) is an unknown parameter (often assumed to satisfy \(\|\theta_0\|_1\le W\) for a known constant \(W\)), and \(\{z_t\}\) are i.i.d. draws from an unknown distribution with CDF \(F\). After the seller posts a price \(p_t>0\), the seller observes only the binary purchase indicator
\[
y_t = \mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}.
\]
The expected revenue from posting price \(p\) in context \(x\) is
\[
R(p;x) = p\,\mathbb P(v\ge p\mid x)= p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
Define the context-dependent optimal price
\[
p^*(x)\in \arg\max_{p>0} p\bigl(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0)\bigr).
\]
The (pseudo-)regret over horizon \(T\) for a policy producing prices \(p_t\) is
\[
\mathcal R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T \Bigl( p^*(x_t)\bigl(1-F(p^*(x_t)-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) - p_t\bigl(1-F(p_t-x_t^\top\theta_0)\bigr) \Bigr),
\]
and performance is measured by \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]\) over the randomness in the data and any policy randomization. The model is "distribution-free" in the sense that \(F\) is unknown and not restricted to a known parametric/log-concave family; the paper assumes regularity such as Lipschitz continuity of \(F\) in some analyses.

Characterize the minimax-optimal regret rate in \(T\) for contextual dynamic pricing under the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) with unknown \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise CDF \(F\), given only binary purchase feedback \(y_t=\mathbf 1\{v_t\ge p_t\}\). In particular, determine whether there exists a policy with expected cumulative regret \(\mathbb E[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) (up to logarithmic factors), or prove a matching lower bound (up to logs) showing \(\inf_{\pi}\sup_{\theta_0,F}\mathbb E_\pi[\mathcal R_T]=\tilde\Omega(T^{2/3})\) (or the correct exponent), over an appropriate class of admissible \(F\) consistent with the paper's assumptions.
location_in_paper: Page 4 (Introduction, end of Abstract/Contributions discussion) and page 20 (Remark 2 following Theorem 1 in Section 3).
open_problem_quote:
Because our problems involves both unknown linear parameter \(\theta_0\) and unknown noise distribution \(F\), we conjecture the obtained \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) rate is close to the optimal rate.

However, their constructed instances do not fit into our considered pricing problem with an additional linear structure \(x^\top\theta_0\). The additional unknown \(\theta_0\) makes the lower bound derivation harder and we leave it for future work.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The open question is a minimax-rate characterization rather than a concrete finite statement, but automated search can explore hard instance families (choices of \(F\), context sequences, and \(\theta_0\)) to empirically stress-test candidate algorithms and suggest lower-bound constructions. Verifying regret for a fixed policy/instance is simulable, but proving minimax optimality is not directly decidable by search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate policies and hard-instance generators can be represented programmatically, with fitness signals given by simulated regret over horizons across sampled or adversarially generated \((\theta_0,F)\) and contexts. This could help discover improved algorithms or plausible lower-bound instance patterns, though translating findings into rigorous minimax proofs would remain substantial theoretical work.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Luo, Sun, and Liu, 2023) formulates contextual dynamic pricing under the linear valuation model \(v_t=x_t^\top\theta_0+z_t\) with only binary purchase feedback and an unknown noise CDF \(F\). Their DIstribution-free Pricing (DIP) policy uses episodic estimation of \(\theta_0\) via a classification reduction (logistic regression on \((1,x_t,p_t)\)) and then, within each episode, discretizes the residual price axis \(p-x_t^\top\hat\theta\) to learn \(1-F\) on a grid using an optimism/UCB rule. A key conceptual contribution is the perturbed linear bandit (PLB) reduction: within an episode, the unknown \(F\) appears as a linear bandit parameter vector \(\xi^*=(1-F(m_1),\dots,1-F(m_d))\), while the error \(\|\hat\theta-\theta_0\|_1\) induces a perturbation level \(C_p\) that necessarily contributes a linear-in-\(T\) term in the PLB regret.

Under Lipschitz continuity of \(F\) and a local quadratic growth/concavity condition on the revenue function \(p\mapsto p(1-F(p-x^\top\theta_0))\), DIP achieves an expected regret bound with a leading \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) term plus an additional term scaling like \(\sum_k \ell_k\,\mathbb E\|\hat\theta_{k-1}-\theta_0\|_1\). The paper also proves a matching \(\Omega(C_pT)\) lower bound for PLB, indicating that controlling the \(\theta_0\)-estimation error is essential. Relative to earlier contextual pricing work that assumes known \(F\) or a known log-concave family (e.g., Javanmard and Nazerzadeh, 2019), the distribution-free setting fundamentally couples learning \(\theta_0\) and learning \(F\), and the best achievable exponent in \(T\) remains unclear.

At present, there is no forward-citation evidence provided here of a paper that establishes the minimax-optimal regret rate for the fully distribution-free model (unknown \(\theta_0\), unknown nonparametric \(F\), binary feedback). Existing related works either impose stronger smoothness on \(F\) and obtain rates such as \(\tilde O(T^{5/7})\) (as discussed by Luo et al.), or study different models (fully nonparametric Lipschitz demand over \((x,p)\), or adversarial settings) that yield different exponents and do not directly resolve the minimax rate for \(v=x^\top\theta_0+z\). Consequently, the minimax characterization—whether \(\tilde\Theta(T^{2/3})\) is achievable and unavoidable under a natural admissible class of Lipschitz (and possibly locally concave) \(F\)—remains open.

sources.title: Distribution-free Contextual Dynamic Pricing
sources.theorem_or_result: Proposes the DIstribution-free Pricing (DIP) policy for linear contextual dynamic pricing with unknown noise CDF \(F\) and unknown \(\theta_0\), using episodic estimation of \(\theta_0\) via logistic regression and a discretized UCB scheme that learns \(1-F\) on a grid. Proves an expected regret bound \(\tilde O(T^{2/3}) + 4p_{\max}L\sum_{k\ge2} 2^{k-2}\ell_2\,\mathbb E\|\hat\theta_{k-1}-\theta_0\|_1\) under Lipschitz \(F\) and a local quadratic growth/concavity condition on revenue; also develops a perturbed linear bandit (PLB) framework with matching \(\Omega(C_pT)\) dependence on perturbation.
sources.connection: Directly addresses the distribution-free contextual dynamic pricing setting with only binary purchase feedback and unknown \(F\). Establishes a \(\tilde O(T^{2/3})\) main term but leaves open whether this is minimax-optimal (and how the \(\theta_0\)-estimation term behaves minimax-wise).

sources.title: Dynamic Pricing in High-Dimensions
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies contextual dynamic pricing under a linear valuation model with binary purchase feedback when the noise distribution is known (RMLP) or belongs to a known log-concave family (RMLP-2). Provides regret guarantees of order \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) (up to dimension/sparsity factors) under these stronger assumptions by leveraging likelihood-based estimation and confidence sets.
sources.connection: Serves as the main benchmark and motivation: shows that when \(F\) is known/structured, \(\tilde O(\sqrt{T})\) is achievable, highlighting the additional difficulty created by unknown, unrestricted \(F\) in the open problem.

sources.title: A Nonparametric Approach to Contextual Dynamic Pricing
sources.theorem_or_result: Considers contextual dynamic pricing with a general Lipschitz demand function over \((x,p)\) and proposes adaptive binning over the covariate space, achieving regret \(\tilde O\bigl(T^{(2+d_0)/(4+d_0)}\bigr)\) and proving matching lower bounds under their nonparametric model assumptions.
sources.connection: Provides nonparametric lower-bound methodology suggesting exponents above \(1/2\) when learning an unknown function; however, their instances do not directly incorporate the linear-valuation-plus-unknown-noise structure \(v=x^\top\theta_0+z\).

sources.title: Semi-Parametric Dynamic Pricing
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies dynamic pricing with an unknown noise distribution under a semi-parametric/log-linear valuation structure that yields separability between the parametric and nonparametric components, enabling learning with bandit feedback and sublinear regret under their model.
sources.connection: Shows that unknown \(F\) can be handled in special semi-parametric models, but their separability does not hold for \(v=x^\top\theta_0+z\), underscoring why the minimax rate here is nontrivial.

sources.title: Dynamic Pricing with Demand Learning and Reference Effects
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops explore-then-commit style policies for dynamic pricing with unknown noise distribution under strong smoothness assumptions (\(m\)-times continuously differentiable), obtaining regret rates such as \(\tilde O\bigl(T^{(2m+1)/(4m-1)}\bigr)\) (e.g., \(\tilde O(T^{5/7})\) for \(m=2\)).
sources.connection: Cited in the source paper as addressing similar unknown-noise pricing; provides alternative rates under stronger smoothness, but not a minimax characterization for the linear contextual binary-feedback model here.

sources.title: Adversarial Dynamic Pricing with Contextual Information
sources.theorem_or_result: Considers an adversarial contextual dynamic pricing setting and proposes a policy ("D2-EXP4") with regret \(\tilde O(T^{3/4})\) under their adversarial/nonstochastic assumptions.
sources.connection: Shows achievable regret in a harder adversarial model but does not settle the stochastic i.i.d.-noise minimax rate for the distribution-free linear valuation model with unknown \(F\).
